items.title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 — now the default for every Free and Pro user
items.summary: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 on June 30 and made it the default model for every Free and Pro user the next day. It performs close to the flagship Opus 4.8 on many tasks, with introductory API pricing of $2/$10 per million tokens through August 31.
items.lifeIdea: Open Claude today and re-run a task that used to feel slow or mediocre — the default model just got a major free upgrade.
items.sublimeAngle: Sonnet 5's introductory pricing window (until Aug 31) is the cheapest moment to prototype any AI feature Sublime might ship.

items.title: US lifts export controls on Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5; global access restoring
items.summary: The U.S. Department of Commerce lifted the June export-control directive that had forced Anthropic to disable its top-tier Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models worldwide. Access began restoring globally on July 1.
items.lifeIdea: If a tool you rely on is Claude-powered, check its model picker today — the strongest tier may have quietly reappeared.
items.sublimeAngle: Regulation can switch a frontier model off overnight — design Sublime's AI features to be model-agnostic from day one.

items.title: South Korea unveils $880 billion, 10-year national AI and semiconductor plan
items.summary: President Lee Jae-myung announced a 1,350-trillion-won (~$880B) investment plan spanning ten years, targeting semiconductors, AI infrastructure and robotics. It is the largest national AI commitment announced to date.
items.lifeIdea: Spend 15 minutes reading how nations are betting on AI infrastructure — it is the clearest signal of where tools and jobs head next.
items.sublimeAngle: Government-scale AI spending eventually means cheaper compute and more open infrastructure — factor that into Sublime's cost roadmap.

items.title: California partners with Anthropic to give Claude to every state agency
items.summary: Governor Newsom announced a partnership making Claude the first AI productivity tool available to all California state agencies, cities and counties — at a 50% discount with free workforce training. It is one of the largest public-sector AI rollouts so far.
items.lifeIdea: Steal the playbook: pick one repetitive task in your week and write a reusable prompt for it, the way state workers are now being trained to.
items.sublimeAngle: Public-sector adoption normalizes AI for mainstream users — Sublime can ride that wave instead of educating the market alone.

items.title: Wired: Meta ran fake-teen 'Cannes' operation to probe rival chatbots with crisis prompts
items.summary: Wired reported that Meta contractors created fake under-18 accounts and systematically sent crisis prompts about self-harm and drugs to rival chatbots including ChatGPT, Gemini and Character.AI, logging the responses. The report raises hard questions about AI safety-testing ethics.
items.lifeIdea: Try one sensitive-topic prompt on your own AI assistant today — knowing exactly where its guardrails sit makes you a safer, sharper user.
items.sublimeAngle: Safety failures are front-page news now — visible guardrails in Sublime's AI features are a trust feature, not overhead.

items.title: OpenAI ships the GPT-5.6 family: Sol, Terra and Luna
items.summary: OpenAI launched the GPT-5.6 model family with three variants — Sol, Terra and Luna — pairing stronger cybersecurity capability with what it calls its most robust safety stack yet. It lands only weeks after GPT-5.5 Instant became the default.
items.lifeIdea: Ask the same question to two different AI models today and note where they disagree — that gap is exactly where your judgment still matters.
items.sublimeAngle: Frontier releases now land every few weeks — Sublime should re-benchmark models quarterly and never hard-code a single vendor.

items.title: GPT-5.5 Instant cuts hallucinations by 52.5% in medicine, law and finance
items.summary: OpenAI made GPT-5.5 Instant its primary default model, reporting a 52.5% reduction in hallucinations across high-stakes medical, legal and financial questions. Reliability, not raw intelligence, is becoming the headline metric.
items.lifeIdea: Ask an AI a factual question from a field you know deeply and fact-check it — calibrate how far to trust it in fields you can't check.
items.sublimeAngle: If Sublime touches anything high-stakes, hallucination benchmarks matter more than leaderboard IQ — evaluate for reliability first.

items.title: Zyphra releases ZAYA1-8B fully open under Apache 2.0, trained on AMD hardware
items.summary: Zyphra released ZAYA1-8B, an open-weight model with a sparse routing architecture — 8B total parameters but only 760M active per token — trained entirely on AMD Instinct hardware. Apache 2.0 licensing means anyone can use it commercially, for free.
items.lifeIdea: Install Ollama (free, no account needed) and run a small open model on your own laptop tonight — private AI with zero subscription.
items.sublimeAngle: Efficient open models like this could power Sublime features at near-zero inference cost, with no API bills and full data privacy.

items.title: Code-first agents go mainstream: Hugging Face's smolagents fits in ~1,000 lines
items.summary: Hugging Face's smolagents library is leading a shift toward minimal, code-first agent frameworks — its core routing logic fits in roughly 1,000 lines of Python and lets models write and execute code in a managed sandbox. Simple agent stacks are winning over heavyweight frameworks.
items.lifeIdea: Read the smolagents README for 10 minutes — understanding one small agent framework demystifies the entire 'AI agents' buzzword.
items.sublimeAngle: Tiny agent runtimes mean a solo founder can ship agentic features — Sublime doesn't need a platform team to automate workflows.

items.title: Chamath Palihapitiya goes full-time CEO at 8090 Labs after $135M Series A
items.summary: Chamath Palihapitiya announced he is taking the full-time CEO role at 8090 Labs, coinciding with a $135M Series A led by Salesforce Ventures. Investors keep concentrating their bets on AI-native software companies.
items.lifeIdea: Skim one AI funding announcement today and ask: what pain point did they get funded to solve? It's free market research.
items.sublimeAngle: AI-native startups raise big on the promise of software that builds itself — position Sublime's story inside that frame.
